risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Families were stratified at each tribal community on the basis of child BMI percentile for age and gender, with two strata at each community: families with children BMIs < 85th percentile and families with children BMIs ≥ 85th percentile. Within each stratum, half of the families were randomly assigned to the intervention condition and half to the control condition. Furthermore, within each stratum, a blocked randomization strategy was used to ensure that there was an equal number of families in the intervention and control groups." Comment: This describes stratified randomization with blocked randomization, which indicates a proper random allocation sequence rather than a predictable or non-random process.

Summary: The paper states that families were randomized after consent and baseline measures, with stratified and blocked randomization by community and BMI percentile, but it gives no explicit description of how the upcoming assignment was concealed from recruiters/enrollers. Comment: Because randomization method is described without any concealment mechanism such as central allocation or sealed opaque envelopes, foreknowledge by the recruiter cannot be ruled out, so the domain remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Intervention group: Year 1 home visits and group sessions... During Year 1, intervention families were also invited..." and "Control group: education only... During Year 1, control families received educational tool kits and incentives sent home on the same cycle as intervention families, but they did not receive any mentoring." Comment: Participants and the personnel delivering the intervention (mentors) could not be blinded to assignment because the intervention involved obvious additional home visits, group sessions, and mentoring versus education only. The outcomes include child BMI, diet, physical activity, screen time, and caregiver behaviors/psychosocial measures, all of which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation and differential interaction with mentors, so this domain is at high risk of performance bias.

Summary: The study does not explicitly state that outcome assessors were blinded, but the main outcomes are objective and measured by study personnel or automated systems: anthropometrics were measured with a stadiometer and calibrated scale, dietary recalls were analyzed using NDSR, physical activity was measured using Actical accelerometers, and biochemical outcomes were laboratory measures. The report describes trained study personnel collecting 24-hour recalls and objective measurement methods, with no indication that knowledge of allocation could influence these measurements. Comment: Because the outcomes reported here are predominantly objective and protocol-driven, lack of explicit blinding is unlikely to have introduced detection bias.

Summary: The provided full text is a study design/methods paper and does not report participant flow or outcome-specific missing-data numbers by arm for the analysed time points. It notes a recruitment goal “to retain 120 families” and acknowledges “the difficulty in retaining families for 2 years,” but gives no actual attrition rates, withdrawals, exclusions, or missing outcome counts for the outcomes of interest. Comment: Because no per-arm loss-to-follow-up data or missing-data handling results are reported in the paper excerpt, the risk of attrition bias cannot be judged confidently.
